system_prompt:
You are distilling one chapter of a guitar method book into a short summary.

The user will give you a chapter file with topic-organized verbatim quotes
plus the human-rationale notes for each. Write a one-paragraph summary (4-7
sentences) that captures:

  - what this chapter teaches (the techniques, concepts, rules, examples)
  - the master's characteristic moves visible in this chapter (if any)
  - the load-bearing terminology this chapter introduces

Reference the topic labels from the chapter file when useful. Do not invent
information not present in the chapter. If the chapter is biographical or
introductory rather than instructional, say so explicitly.

Output just the paragraph — no preamble, no JSON, no markdown formatting.
user_prompt:
Chapter 11: Dissonance, Chromatic Riffs, Diminished and Altered Minor (Lessons 71-79)

<<<CHAPTER FILE
# Chapter 11 — Dissonance, Chromatic Riffs, Diminished and Altered Minor (Lessons 71-79)

_Pages 145-162._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Dominant b5 polyphonic principle

> If we analyze the E749 chord in the diagram below, we can see it contains E, G#, D, and G—the 1, 3, 57, and #9—of the key of
> E major. If we analyze the Bb135 chord, we see it contains Bb, E, Gé, D, and G—the 1,5, 3, +7, and 13 of the key of Bb major.
> What's the only difference between these two chords? The B root note of the second chord! B> happens to be the }5 of the
> key of E, which is the cornerstone and principle of this lesson.

**Significance (p. 145).** Tritone-sub cornerstone: chord on b5 shares tones with original.

## Four tension tones rule

> Bob Conti used to
> tell me there were four tension tones—5, #5, 59, 49—and that's it. The rest of the tones are already in the dominant key that
> you're in, Learning to work those tension tones is what it’s all about.

**Significance (p. 145).** Exact inventory: four tension tones outside native dominant scale.

## Bb Mixolydian defined

> Measure 1: We're moving straight up through B> Mixolydian beginning with the B note. | call that Bb a lead-in tone—
> the B note being a half step lower than C. B+ Mixolydian is the B> major scale with a 47th instead of a natural major 7th.
> That's why they call it a dominant seven or +7.

**Significance (p. 145).** Defines Mixolydian and lead-in tone concept.

## Bb diminished scale formula

> Measure 1: All the notes of this bar can be related to the B> diminished scale. Do you know it? If not, you'd better learn
> it: half step, whole step, half step, whole step, etc.

**Significance (p. 145).** H-W-H-W formula for octatonic diminished scale.

## G super Locrian / Db blues scale

> Measure 2: G diminished going into a G super Locrian connecting with the next bar. (Ab melodic minor has the same
> notes as the G super Locrian scale.)

**Significance (p. 147).** Enharmonic identity bridging Lessons 78 and 79.

## Chromatic ascending riffs technique

> You can take any riff and repeat it chromatically up the fretboard a half step (one fret) at a time. | got this idea by watching
> fusion players like John Abercrombie and John Scofield. The trick is to resolve the line by ending on notes that lie firmly in key.
> You can target notes up the neck and end up there.

**Significance (p. 148).** Half-step displacement + resolution to in-key targets.

## Whole-step chromatic variation

> You can take virtually any riff and apply this line of thinking. Try moving up whole steps (two frets at a time). You can create
> an interesting moment of tension in the middle of a solo.

**Significance (p. 149).** Whole-step variation creates mid-solo tension.

## Descending chromatic riff resolution rule

> In all your creative efforts to come up with melodies, it’s important to know you can try and reverse every idea to see what
> else you can think of. If you can play ascending chromatic riffs, for example, you can also play descending chromatic riffs.
> The important thing to be aware of is how you resolve and finish these lines.

**Significance (p. 150).** Symmetric ascending/descending; resolution is non-negotiable.

## Diminished scale construction and key equivalence

> A C diminished scale contains C-Db—E)-E-F#-G-A-B», or the 149-29 (or b3)-3-45
> (or #4)-5-6-47. We can construct a basic C7 chord using the 1, 3, 5, and+7 of this scale. If we stick to the theory that we can play
> the scale over any chord we created from it, then the C diminished scale will work over the C7 chord.

**Significance (p. 152).** Note content + numeric formula + dominant application rule.

## Diminished scale three-fret equivalence

> The same exact patterns and fingerings occur three frets above and below. So this scale will work for C, Dé (or E>), F# (or Gs), and
> A because these keys are three half steps apart.

**Significance (p. 152).** One fingering covers four keys.

## Diminished chord = harmonic minor

> Since a diminished chord can be found in the harmonic minor scale,
> we can also use the harmonic minor scale over the diminished chord. Here's a breakdown of triads in the A harmonic minor
> scale:
>
> i iie biH+ iv Vv VI viie
> Am Be C+ Dm E F Ge

**Significance (p. 154).** Harmonic-minor triad chart justifies scale-over-chord rule.

## Diminished chord three-fret symmetry

> If we analyze the diminished chord, we find that it repeats itself every three half steps. In other words, B, D, F, and G# are
> all three half steps (or frets) apart, and the chords are built from the same four notes. In these examples, both the B and G#
> diminished chords could be thought of as the same chord (B° = B-D-F-G# and Ge = G¢-B-D-F). Both chords have the same
> notes; they're just in a different order.

**Significance (p. 154).** Only three distinct diminished-chord shapes exist.

## Melodic minor scale formula

> The A melodic minor scale is simply the A major scale with a flat or minor 3rd: 1-2-+43-4-5-6-7. The same harmonic applications
> apply to the melodic minor scale. Let’s look at a harmonized A melodic minor scale in seventh chords:
>
> i ii” oill+ IV Vv vi vii”
> Am(maj7) Bm715 Cmaj7+ D7 E7 Fem7+5 Gim7)5

**Significance (p. 158).** Major-with-flat-3 formula + seventh-chord harmonization.

## Super Locrian formula and Ab melodic minor identity

> The super Locrian mode for G is G-Ab~-Be-G.-D>-B-F. Numerically,
> that looks like 1+2-43-3-45-46-47. It basically has all the altered notes: >}5, 25, 59, and #9. It’s also important to note the 1, 3, and
> »7, which create a dominant seven chord without the perfect 5th tone of the scale. The example below is to show you that the
> A» melodic minor scale contains the same notes as the G super Locrian scale. We could say that the G super Locrian scale is the
> seventh mode of Ab melodic minor. Most musicians think of the super Locrian scale as a melodic minor scale that's built a half
> step higher than the root of the altered dominant chord over which you're playing.

**Significance (p. 160).** Complete super Locrian formula + Ab melodic minor identity.

CHAPTER FILE>>>
